# LangGraph Orchestration Architecture

Orchestration patterns for this stack (TypeScript + Python, Next.js/React frontend, LangGraph agent runtime, Playwright). LangGraph is a *low-level, stateful* runtime: you own the graph, state shape, and control flow. Default to **concept-first reasoning + the official docs**, not memorized API — the API surface moved hosts and is still shifting (see Gotchas).

## Pull current docs first

The docs **moved** from `langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph` to `docs.langchain.com`. Always fetch the current page before relying on an API name:

- Overview: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/overview
- Graph API (StateGraph, reducers, Command, Send): https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/graph-api
- Persistence (checkpointers, threads, store, time-travel): https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/persistence
- Durable execution (durability modes, replay): https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/durable-execution
- Human-in-the-loop (`interrupt()` / `Command(resume=...)`): https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/interrupts
- Streaming (`stream_mode`): https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/streaming
- Tools / `ToolNode` / `ToolRuntime`: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/tools
- Multi-agent patterns: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/multi-agent
- API reference: https://reference.langchain.com/python/langgraph/
- TS port (LangGraph.js, for the Next.js side): https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraphjs/

Use Context7 (`/langchain-ai/langgraph`) for live API signatures when a doc page is ambiguous.

## State & graph design

- **State is a typed channel map**, not a blob. Define it with `TypedDict`/Pydantic (Python) or `Annotation`/Zod (TS). Each key is a *channel*; a **reducer** (`Annotated[list, add_messages]`) decides merge-vs-overwrite. No reducer = last-write-wins, which silently clobbers parallel updates.
- **Keep state minimal.** Persist heavy artifacts (evidence bundles, media, screenshots) to object storage and keep only IDs/pointers + URIs in graph state. Checkpointers serialize the *entire* state on every super-step — large blobs in state = slow, expensive, and bloat the DB.
- **Prefer several small graphs over one mega-graph.** Compose with subgraphs and pass IDs between them. Small graphs are easier to test, resume, and reason about; a four-graph split (intelligence → content → media → learning) keeps each interrupt/resume boundary clean.
- **`Command` unifies update + route**: return `Command(update={...}, goto="next")` from a node instead of splitting logic across a node and a separate conditional edge. Use `add_conditional_edges` when routing is a pure function of state with no update.
- **`Send` for fan-out / map-reduce**: parallel artifact generation (blog, one-pager, social, demo script) is a `Send`-based fan-out into one worker node, with an `add_messages`-style reducer to gather results. Don't hand-roll parallelism in a single node.

## Tool wiring

- Define tools with the `@tool` decorator (type hints = input schema, docstring = description). Execute them with the prebuilt **`ToolNode`**; route the agent loop with **`tools_condition`**.
- For a standard agentic loop, start from the prebuilt factory (`create_agent` / `create_react_agent`) and only drop to a hand-built `StateGraph` when you need custom routing, interrupts mid-loop, or non-LLM nodes.
- Tools reach runtime context (state, store, thread/run IDs, retry count) via the injected **`ToolRuntime`** param — it's hidden from the LLM. Don't smuggle context through globals.
- Wrap external/deterministic side effects (git diff, GitHub API, Playwright, ffmpeg, S3, Bedrock) so they're **idempotent on replay** — see durable execution below.

## Persistence, interrupts & handoffs (the approval-gate core)

- A **checkpointer is mandatory** for interrupts, resume, and durability. Dev: `InMemorySaver`. Prod: `PostgresSaver`/`AsyncPostgresSaver` (point it at the project's Postgres — e.g. Aurora). Run the saver's `.setup()` migration once before first use.
- Every run is keyed by **`thread_id`** in `config={"configurable": {"thread_id": ...}}`. Same thread_id = continue that conversation/run; new id = fresh state. The dashboard must store and replay the *same* thread_id to resume.
- **Human approval = dynamic `interrupt(payload)`** inside a node. It pauses, persists, and surfaces the payload (`result["__interrupt__"]` for `invoke`, or on the stream). Resume by re-invoking with **`Command(resume=value)`** on the same thread_id — the resumed value becomes the `interrupt()` return value. Use this for the feature-manifest / artifact / skill-revision gates; surface a JSON payload with a dashboard URL.
- **The interrupted node re-runs from its start on resume.** Put `interrupt()` early in the node, or split it out, so code before it isn't re-executed with side effects.
- `interrupt_before` / `interrupt_after` at compile time are **static breakpoints for debugging**, not the production approval mechanism — don't confuse them with `interrupt()`.
- **Cross-thread memory** (skill snapshots, learning signals, reviewer-edit history) belongs in the **`Store`** interface (`PostgresStore` + pgvector), not in per-thread checkpoints. Checkpoints are scoped to one thread; the Store is shared.
- **Handoffs**: transfer control with `Command(goto=..., graph=...)`, the supervisor/router pattern, or subagents-as-tools. Pick per the multi-agent doc; for a fixed pipeline, explicit edges beat a dynamic supervisor (cheaper, deterministic, testable).

## Durable execution & cost/latency control

- **Durability modes** trade safety for speed: `"sync"` (write before each step — safest, slowest), `"async"` (write while next step runs — balanced), `"exit"` (write only at completion/error/interrupt — fastest, loses mid-run state on crash). Long GitHub-Actions jobs that must survive a crash → `"sync"` or `"async"`; cheap fast graphs → `"exit"`.
- **Nodes replay on recovery** — they must be idempotent and deterministic. Wrap non-deterministic work (LLM calls, network, randomness, timestamps) so re-execution doesn't double-charge or double-write. Use the framework's `task`/checkpointed-side-effect mechanism where available; otherwise guard with an idempotency key persisted in state/store.
- **Cost**: route by difficulty (cheap model for extraction/classification, capable model for generation/eval); cache deterministic extractor outputs by content hash; cap fan-out width; set a sane `recursion_limit` so a routing bug can't loop forever and rack up tokens.
- **Latency**: fan out independent artifact generation with `Send` rather than sequentially; stream with `stream_mode="messages"` for token UX and `"updates"` for step progress to the dashboard; run deterministic policy checks *before* the expensive LLM-as-judge / Guardrails pass so cheap rejections short-circuit early.
- **Eval gates as graph nodes**: make checks first-class nodes with conditional edges (deterministic policy check → LLM/Guardrails eval → route to approve-interrupt vs. regenerate). Gate downstream generation on passing; never generate marketing claims directly from raw diffs — gate on an approved, evidence-linked manifest first.

## Checklist

- [ ] State schema is typed, minimal, and every concurrently-written channel has an explicit reducer.
- [ ] Heavy artifacts live in object storage; only IDs/URIs are in graph state.
- [ ] A real checkpointer is compiled in (Postgres/Aurora in prod) and its migration has run.
- [ ] Every run threads a stable `thread_id`; the dashboard persists it to resume.
- [ ] Approval gates use dynamic `interrupt()` + `Command(resume=...)`, not static breakpoints.
- [ ] Interrupting nodes are safe to re-run from the top (no pre-interrupt side effects).
- [ ] Cross-thread/long-term data uses the `Store`, not checkpoints.
- [ ] Non-deterministic side effects are idempotent on replay; durability mode matches the job's crash-tolerance.
- [ ] Parallelizable work uses `Send`/fan-out; `recursion_limit` is set.
- [ ] Eval/guardrail checks are nodes with conditional routing; cheap deterministic checks run before expensive ones.
- [ ] Verified API names against the current docs above (not training memory).

## Gotchas

- **Stale URLs / training memory.** Docs moved to `docs.langchain.com`; the old `langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph` pages redirect. The API is mid-evolution (`create_react_agent` → `create_agent`, `ToolRuntime` replacing older injection, stream `version` formats). Confirm names against the fetched page or Context7 — do not trust memorized signatures.
- **No checkpointer ⇒ interrupts silently don't work.** `interrupt()` requires persistence; with `InMemorySaver` your approval gates evaporate when the GitHub-Actions runner exits. Resume must use the *same* `thread_id` against a *durable* store.
- **The interrupted node re-executes from its beginning on resume**, not from the `interrupt()` line. Any side effect before the interrupt runs twice. Put `interrupt()` first or isolate it.
- **Missing reducers corrupt parallel/fan-out state.** Two `Send` branches writing the same key with no reducer = one silently overwrites the other. `add_messages` matches/updates by message ID — appending dict-shaped messages without stable IDs causes duplicates or lost updates.
- **Fat state kills performance and the DB.** The whole state serializes every super-step. Putting evidence bundles, screenshots, or media bytes in state (instead of S3 URIs) makes checkpoints huge and slow and can blow past row/size limits.
- **`exit` durability loses mid-run progress on crash.** A long runner job that crashes mid-pipeline with `durability="exit"` restarts from the last *completed* invoke, not the last step. For multi-minute jobs use `async`/`sync`.
- **Non-idempotent nodes double-charge on replay.** Recovery/time-travel re-runs nodes; un-guarded Bedrock calls, S3 writes, or GitHub mutations fire twice. Guard with idempotency keys persisted in state/store.
- **Checkpoints ≠ long-term memory.** Checkpoints are per-`thread_id`; data that must outlive or cross threads (skill snapshots, learning ledger, reviewer edits) belongs in the `Store` (+ pgvector), or it's invisible to other runs.
- **Unbounded loops burn tokens.** A buggy conditional edge or supervisor handoff can loop until the default `recursion_limit` trips — set it explicitly and treat hitting it as an error to investigate, not a config to bump.
- **Async/sync mismatch.** Mixing `astream`/`AsyncPostgresSaver` with sync nodes (or vice-versa) deadlocks or silently runs synchronously. Keep the whole graph + saver on one paradigm.
- **Python ↔ TS drift.** LangGraph.js (Next.js side) and LangGraph (Python worker) are separate ports with their own release cadences and small API differences. Don't assume a Python pattern names map 1:1 to TS — check the JS docs for the frontend-side graph.
- _Stack-specific runtime gotchas beyond the above: none observed yet._
